For enterprise participants watching OpenAI's governance evolve, Zilis's testimony underscores how personal relationships and undisclosed conflicts shape high-stakes institutional decisions. The case demonstrates that formal governance structures can be undermined by informal channels—a pattern relevant to any organization where key advisors operate across competing interests without transparent disclosure.
items.original_title: Musk’s biggest loyalist became his biggest liability

items.headline: Anthropic Doubles Claude Code Limits With SpaceX Compute Deal
items.body:
Anthropic announced a compute infrastructure deal with SpaceX on Wednesday that grants access to over 300 megawatts of new capacity from SpaceX's Memphis data center. The partnership immediately enables the company to raise usage limits for paying subscribers—doubling the five-hour window for Claude Code on Pro and Max plans and removing peak-hours throttling. CEO Dario Amodei disclosed the arrangement at the company's developer conference, framing it as a direct response to surging demand that has outpaced available compute resources.

## The Infrastructure Play

SpaceX's contribution centers on Colossus 1, a supercomputer housing over 220,000 NVIDIA GPUs including H100, H200, and next-generation GB200 accelerators. Anthropic also raised API limits for its Opus model, though the company did not specify exact new thresholds in its announcement. This deal adds to a reported wave of infrastructure agreements Anthropic signed last month with Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Nvidia—partnerships the company credits for enabling the current increases. Beyond immediate capacity gains, Anthropic has expressed interest in collaborating with SpaceX on "multiple gigawatts" of orbital compute infrastructure, positioning itself within the emerging but unproven concept of space-based data centers as a solution to terrestrial power and cooling constraints.

## Resolving A Visible Bottleneck

Anthropic has faced sustained developer frustration over usage caps. Discussions on Hacker News, Reddit, and X have centered on limits preventing extended work sessions. The company's recent moves to introduce peak-hour throttling and reportedly test removing Claude Code from its $20 Pro tier generated vocal backlash in developer communities. The new limits represent an operational necessity rather than strategic choice—demand has shifted toward multi-agent workflows and professional adoption in software development organizations, while concurrent migration from OpenAI following military contract controversies has intensified pressure on Anthropic's compute budget.

The timing also marks a public reconciliation with Elon Musk, who criticized Anthropic in February with unfounded claims about the company's constitutional AI practices. Musk reversed his position Wednesday, stating he spent time with Anthropic leadership and was "impressed" by their safety approach. For enterprise AI practitioners, the announcement signals that compute constraints remain the primary limiting factor in LLM availability and capability scaling. The partnership underscores that infrastructure capacity—not model capability itself—is currently the bottleneck determining whether advanced AI tools reach production workloads at meaningful scale.
items.original_title: Anthropic raises Claude Code usage limits, credits new deal with SpaceX

items.headline: TSMC locks in 1GW wind deal as Taiwan faces energy squeeze from AI demand
items.body:
TSMC has signed a 30-year corporate power purchase agreement to take 100 percent of output from the Hai Long offshore wind project, securing over 1 gigawatt of capacity across three wind sites off Taiwan's central western coast. The deal with Canada-based Northland Power marks a significant bet on renewable energy as the chipmaker grapples with mounting electricity demands and Taiwan confronts a widening energy shortfall.

## The Power Purchase Pipeline

The Hai Long project began supplying power to Taiwan's grid in 2025 and will reach full capacity by 2027, with enough generation to power the equivalent of 1 million Taiwanese households. This agreement joins two prior renewable deals TSMC struck: a 2020 contract with Danish firm Ørsted for 920 megawatts from the Greater Changhua offshore wind farm, expected operational by late 2026, and a 2021 commitment with German developer WPD for over 1 gigawatt of onshore and offshore wind capacity.

Taiwan's energy crisis has intensified sharply since March 2026, when Iranian drone strikes damaged Qatar's natural gas facilities, severing a critical LNG supply line that had provided one-third of Taiwan's typical gas imports. Taiwan generates roughly half its electricity from natural gas plants and maintains only two weeks of fuel reserves. The government has extended immediate supply through August or September by sourcing from Australia and the United States, but the vulnerability is acute: Taiwan imports nearly 97 percent of its energy needs.

## Computing Demand Reshaping Grid Requirements

TSMC consumed nearly 10 percent of Taiwan's total electricity in 2023. More critically, S&P Global estimates that share will climb to approximately 25 percent by 2030 as the chipmaker expands energy-intensive fabs to meet global AI chip demand. This trajectory forces Taiwan's hand: the government has accelerated plans to restart shuttered nuclear plants and is targeting 15 gigawatts of offshore wind capacity by 2035. TSMC itself has committed to 60 percent renewable energy across global operations by 2030 and 100 percent by 2040.

The interplay is structural, not incidental. Taiwan's position as the critical supplier of advanced AI chips—and the energy-intensive nature of modern semiconductor manufacturing—means grid stability and decarbonization are now inseparable from geopolitical resilience. For enterprise customers depending on TSMC capacity, the energy equation is a real constraint on supply expansion. Taiwan's ability to secure stable power directly determines global AI infrastructure growth.
items.original_title: TSMC taps wind power as AI chip demand soars, Taiwan feels energy crunch
